title: HPE Delivers Upgraded HPC Hardware, Software For Security, Sovereignty, And Multi-Tenancy
gist: HPE used ISC High Performance 2026 to launch new multi-tenancy and security software for its Cray and ProLiant HPC platforms, including a revamped Slingshot 400 interconnect with secure tenant isolation and a unified Supercomputing Programming Software environment. The company's HPC chief Trish Damkroger frames the push as a direct response to sovereign AI demand and the growing threat surface introduced by autonomous AI agents. HPE positions security and data sovereignty as a structural, long-term growth theme as governments and enterprises accelerate AI adoption.

insights.claim: Over the past twelve months HPC customer conversations shifted from generative AI to sovereign AI — organizations wanting to own their AI infrastructure, keep data local, and comply with government regulations.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Sovereign AI is now a primary commercial driver for HPC vendors, expanding the addressable market beyond pure compute performance to compliance and data-residency solutions.
insights.quote: Around this time last year, most of the AI talk that Trish Damkroger heard revolved around generative AI... But that changed in the last twelve months, with the focus shifting to sovereign AI.

insights.claim: Sovereign AI introduces new security layers beyond traditional air-gapping, with autonomous AI agents running jobs without human intervention creating a novel internal threat vector.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Vendors that can provide agent-level access control and classification frameworks stand to win security-sensitive HPC contracts as agentic workloads grow.
insights.quote: Sovereign is still about control, compliance, and security, but AI adds so many more layers of threat.

insights.claim: The classification and access control of autonomous AI agents running on HPC systems is now a top security concern for HPE's customers.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Organizations deploying agentic AI on sensitive infrastructure need new identity and access management paradigms, creating opportunity for security-focused software layers.
insights.quote: With AI, you could put together the infrastructure, maybe you air-gap it, maybe you do other things to control who has access to it, but what about the agents?

insights.claim: HPE launched new Supercomputing Programming Software at ISC 2026 that integrates vendor, open-source, and HPE tools into a single containerized programming environment, covering HPE's ProLiant DL and XD server lines in addition to Cray systems.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Extending HPC software tooling to ProLiant servers broadens HPE's addressable market from pure supercomputing to enterprise AI servers, potentially compressing cycles for customers migrating from HPC to AI training/inference.

insights.claim: HPE released a new Slingshot 400 software update enabling multi-tenancy with secure user separation and restricted unauthorized routing, and the update can be applied to already-deployed switches.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Retrofit-capable security software upgrades extend the revenue lifecycle of already-installed Slingshot infrastructure and lower customer switching costs away from competing interconnects.

insights.claim: The Slingshot 400 switch ASIC provides 51.2 Tb/sec aggregate bandwidth with 64 ports at 400 Gb/sec (expandable to 128 at 200 Gb/sec or 256 at 100 Gb/sec) and supports 260,000 endpoints in a three-level network.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: At 260,000-endpoint scale, Slingshot 400 claims a lead over InfiniBand for large cluster deployments, which matters for national labs and hyperscale AI training build-outs.
insights.quote: HPE says Slingshot 400 can support 260,000 endpoints with a three level network, which is a lot better than the scale of InfiniBand while still being an Ethernet switch.

insights.claim: HPE introduced a new GUI and API for multi-tenancy management of the Cray Supercomputing Storage Systems E2000 parallel file system, targeting large-scale automation.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Easier multi-tenant storage management reduces operational overhead for national labs and cloud HPC providers hosting multiple research groups or tenants on shared infrastructure.

insights.claim: HPE's November 2025 SC25 announcement already covered new Cray Supercomputing GX5000 blades, the K3000 storage system, and Slingshot 400 in the hardware architecture; ISC 2026 focus is therefore squarely on software.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: HPE's cadence — hardware at SC, software at ISC — signals a maturing platform strategy where software differentiation is increasingly the competitive battleground.

themes.theme: Convergence of HPC, AI, and Quantum

insights.claim: Quantum computing is now being added to the HPC and AI convergence that HPE and its customers are planning for, adding another dimension to the multi-tenant security architecture challenge.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Early movers building quantum-ready HPC platforms with multi-tenant security may capture long-term government and research contracts as quantum hardware matures.

insights.claim: Multi-tenancy is the default operating model for most HPC customers — including national labs — not an edge case, requiring that security and isolation run through compute, networking, and storage simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Point solutions that secure only compute will fail; vendors offering stack-wide multi-tenancy — like HPE's current push — have a structural advantage for winning full-system contracts.
insights.quote: Multi-tendency is what most of our customers have, and even if you're at a national lab, you have different users trying to use the system.

themes.theme: AI Threat Landscape and Regulatory Pace

insights.claim: AI tools being adopted by threat actors create a symmetric risk for infrastructure operators: the same advances that improve productivity also accelerate adversarial capabilities against that infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Security spending in HPC and AI infrastructure is structurally non-discretionary; demand will grow proportionally with AI adoption by both defenders and attackers.
insights.quote: The new tools are, again, great, but they're also a threat because they're going to be used against you.

insights.claim: Regulatory compliance bodies are struggling to keep pace with the rate of AI innovation, with Damkroger citing the Anthropic-Trump Administration conflict over the Mythos AI and Fable 5 models as an example of the governance challenge.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Regulatory uncertainty around frontier AI models creates both risk (compliance overhead) and opportunity (demand for sovereign, on-premises AI infrastructure that sidesteps cloud data-sharing concerns).
insights.quote: Think about the whole thing with Anthropic [and its conflict with the Trump Administration over the security and use of its Mythos AI and Fable 5 frontier models].

insights.claim: HPE requires hardware-level security down to the core and an external root of trust, working closely with chip vendors Nvidia, AMD, and Intel to achieve this.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Deep silicon-level security integration strengthens HPE's lock-in with its hardware ecosystem partners and raises the bar for pure-software HPC security competitors.
insights.quote: We work very closely with our vendors, Nvidia, AMD, Intel, because you have to have security down to the core.
